title: Chinese Congee (Plain Rice Porridge with Toppings)

ingredients.name: jasmine or ponni rice, rinsed
visual_checks: 1 cup raw rice makes a thick congee for 4; use 1.5 cups for thinner porridge
ingredients.notes: 1 cup raw rice makes a thick congee for 4; use 1.5 cups for thinner porridge

subs.name: 1 cup broken rice or leftover cooked rice
subs.notes: broken rice dissolves faster; cooked rice takes only 20–25 minutes on the stovetop

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: or 6 cups water + 2 cups vegetable stock for extra flavour
ingredients.notes: or 6 cups water + 2 cups vegetable stock for extra flavour

subs.name: fish sauce or a pinch of salt
subs.notes: soy adds colour and umami; fish sauce gives a Cantonese flavour

subs.name: a few drops of mustard oil off heat
subs.notes: sesame oil is worth seeking out for congee — the flavour is distinctive

ingredients.name: neutral cooking oil
visual_checks: add to rice before cooking to prevent sticking
ingredients.notes: add to rice before cooking to prevent sticking

ingredients.name: eggs (for optional topping)
visual_checks: soft-boil or poach and place on top
ingredients.notes: soft-boil or poach and place on top

steps.text: Combine the rinsed rice, water and 1 teaspoon of oil in a heavy-bottomed pot or pressure cooker. The oil keeps the rice from foaming over.

For a stovetop method: bring to a boil over high heat, then reduce to the lowest possible simmer. Cover with the lid slightly ajar and cook for 45–60 minutes, stirring every 15 minutes to prevent the bottom from catching, until the rice has dissolved into a thick, creamy porridge.

For a pressure cooker method (faster): close the lid and cook on high pressure for 20 minutes (6 whistles on a standard Indian cooker). Turn off the heat and allow the pressure to release naturally — about 15 minutes.

Open the cooker and stir vigorously with a ladle — the rice will be very thick at first; it loosens as you stir. If it is too thick, add a little hot water and stir to your preferred consistency. Season with salt.
If adding soft-boiled eggs: while the congee cooks, place eggs in a small saucepan of boiling water and cook for exactly 6 minutes. Transfer to cold water, peel and halve.
Ladle the congee into deep bowls. Drizzle each bowl with soy sauce and sesame oil. Top with julienned ginger and spring onions. Add a halved soft-boiled egg if using and serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice → 1 cup broken rice or leftover cooked rice (broken rice dissolves faster; cooked rice takes only 20–25 minutes on the stovetop); soy-sauce → fish sauce or a pinch of salt (soy adds colour and umami; fish sauce gives a Cantonese flavour); gingelly-oil → a few drops of mustard oil off heat (sesame oil is worth seeking out for congee — the flavour is distinctive)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A pressure cooker is the ideal tool here — 6 whistles on high pressure and 15 minutes natural release gives you perfectly dissolved congee with almost no effort. Use ponni or sona masoori rice. Gingelly oil (nallennai) is your sesame oil. Soy sauce is optional but recommended; find it at Indian grocery stores under the Chings or Maggi label. The result is a warming, easily digestible meal ideal for sick days or cool mornings.
